source_metadata.title_raw: Stephen J. Rooklidge, PhD, PE - A-18-224 - November 6, 2018 - Fall River Mills

content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
• 1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Stephen J. Rooklidge, PhD, PE 
General Manager 
November 6, 2018 
Fall River Valley Community Services District 
PO Box 427 
Fall River Mills, CA 96028 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-224 
Dear Mr. Rooklidge: 
This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-government provisions of the 
Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment 
provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post-
governmental employment laws that may apply, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411, or 
Section 1090. 
Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 
FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is 
not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for 
additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Are you subject to the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act due to your 
former position as a State Water Resources Control Board engineer? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. Your position involved participating in making decisions with foreseeable and material 
effects on financial interests, and you are subject to the one-year ban and the permanent ban under 
the Act. Based upon the facts provided, you may not contact or communicate with the State Water 
Board or any employee of the Board for the purposes of influencing an action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any contract or grant. However, the 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 2 
one-year ban does not prohibit limited communications with the State Water Board or an employee 
of the Board to implement an awarded grant. Permitted communications include seeking 
clarification of technical requirements identified in a grant that has already been awarded. 
Moreover, the one-year ban does not prohibit you from working on a grant on behalf of your 
current agency so long as you do not personally communicate or appear before the State Water 
Board, or an employee of the Board, and you are not identified by name in any documents 
submitted by your agency to the State Water Board related to the award of grant funds or the 
amendment of an existing grant. 
FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER 
In August, 2018 you left state service. You were previously employed by the State Water 
Resources Control Board ("State Water Board"), Division of Drinking Water ("DDW"), Northern 
California Section, Lassen District of the Redding Office.2 You were employed as a Water 
Resource Control Engineer D, and worked as an active regulator of water systems in Sierra and 
Plumas counties for four years. You hold a PhD in Civil Engineering and are a licensed professional 
engineer (PE). Prior to making a iateral transfer to the DDW, you held a similar position with the 
State Water Board related to wastewater treatment for one year. Your DDW engineering duties 
included: 
• 
Onsite inspections of public water-related systems or facilities (such as source intakes, 
storage reservoirs) which involved: 
o Facility inspections and reviews of operational records, procedures, submitted 
documents, and all required administrative programs. 
o Preparing reports and letters documenting inspections and required corrective 
actions. 
o Communicating your findings with the Senior Sanitary Engineer and water system 
personnel. 
o Updating computer databases to track water quality sampling. 
• 
Permit requests for the construction/modification of public water systems which involved 
preparing or reviewing reports and recommendations. 
• 
Reviewing engineering plans and specifications for proposed water system improvements 
and projects, and inspecting construction sites to verify compliance. 
• 
Reviewing lab data to advise water systems on appropriate treatment methods. 
To a lesser extent your position involved consulting and advising health and public officials 
on drinking water programs and research projects on water quality issues for policy development 
and studies. These duties were under the direction of your supervisor, a Senior Engineer. Your 
position with DDW was not included in the agency's conflict-of-interest code. 
In our telephone conversation, you stated that your position as a "regulator" required you to 
review data, make analyses, recommend actions, and justify the recommendations to your 
2 This DOW district included Shasta, Lassen, Plumas, Placer and Sierra counties. 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 3 
supervisor, and to his supervisor, a Principal Engineer. You note that you "did not havl? a free hand" 
to make decisions outright and your supervisor(s) signed all letters of corrective action or notices of 
violation. However, you also state that "your opinion was trusted" by your superiors at DDW. 
Specifically, you described your "regulator" duties as follows: your job was to assure that 
water facilities were in compliance with state law and regulations. To do this, you took samples and 
prepared reports on your results. If a facility, for example, had a positive water sample test 
(indicating a violation) you drafted the "notice of violation" which resulted in a fine. The notice 
would rely on your collection of data, and was subject to a discussion with your supervisor. The 
letters were reviewed and signed by your supervisor. In performing permit reviews, the process 
involved your office conditionally approving the permit, to be followed by your inspection. At that 
time, you would ascertain if the operation was consistent with the required conditions. Based on 
your analysis the new permit would be issued or additional actions would ensue. When reviewing 
engineering plans, you would analyze the plans to determine if the plans lacked the correct 
information, you would raise "red flags" if items were lacking, and point out where pilot studies 
may be needed. Your engineering plan review letters would identify these issues, and explain the 
analysis. The letters were reviewed and signed by your supervisor. Similarly, for lab data you 
gathered the samples, made the tests, and analyzed the results. You state that your recommendations 
for corrective action were discussed with your supervisors and followed. 
Commencing in September, 2018 you are now the General Manager of a Community 
Services District in Shasta County, "Fall River Valley CSD ("Fall River District"). As the new 
General Manager, you are the point of contact for all grants and budgets for wastewater, water, and 
parks within the Fall River District. You were specifically hired to manage these complex project 
duties. The State Water Board regulates the CSD, which is located in your former DDW district. 
You state that you did not regulate Shasta County water systems as a DDW employee, and were not 
involved with any local projects administered by the State. 
The Fall River District has three active "planning" grant projects with the State Water 
Board's Division of Financial Assistance ("DF A"), the funding arm for certain water and 
wastewater grants. The District's Proposition 1-funded grant projects include: a Technical 
Assistance Grant for new water sources (wells), a Prop 1 Water Planning Grant for the design of a 
new water tank and booster pumps, and a Wastewater Planning Grant to extend the area of service 
from Fall River Mills to nearby McArthur, CA. 
A State Water Board DF A official has called into question whether you have a conflict of 
interest to work on the Technical Assistance Grant project for Fall River Valley CSD, because of 
your previous DDW employment. You state that you did not assist the Fall River District in getting 
this particular grant, or any of the above listed grants, either as a technical reviewer or an inspector, 
because your DDW territory was far south of this system. You were never requested to assist any of 
your colleagues in the review of these projects, nor were you involved with grant assistance in any 
way. Additionally, you provided the State Water Board's conflict of interest code, which (as noted 
above) does not list your former position as a designated employee for financial interest disclosure 
purposes. Your understanding is that your position was correctly not designated, as it "wasn't 
suitable for altering policy." 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 4 
You also note that if you are prevented under the Act's one-year ban from participation in 
the grant proceedings it would present a severe hardship for the disadvantaged community you 
serve because the grant projects you currently have would have to be funneled through a Fall River 
District board member who has no experience in project management.3 As a salaried employee of 
this District, you state that you would receive no personal benefit to alter or amend existing 
projects. You anticipate being involved in grant applications to the State Water Board on behalf of 
the Fall River District in the near future. 
ANALYSIS 
Public officials who have left state service are subject to two types of post-governmental 
employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) 
The One-Year Ban 
Section 87406(d)(l) states: 
A designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, 
employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position that 
entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest, and a member of a 
state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or 
employment, shall not, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or 
otherwise represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal 
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before any state 
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked 
or represented during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the 
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing 
administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property .... 
Simply stated, Section 87406 imposes a one-year ban on state employees who made, or 
participated in making, decisions that could have any foreseeable and material financial effect. The 
ban applies for twelve months from the date the employee permanently leaves state office or 
employment. This ban prevents the employee from representing any other person, for pay, before 
their former agency for the purpose of influencing the agency's decisions on general rules, or on 
specific proceedings such as a permit, grant, or contract. (See Section 87406; Regulation 18746.1.) 
Unlike the permanent ban, this ban is not confined to particular proceedings. 
The determining factor in applying the one-year ban is whether the employee holds a 
position that "entails making or participating in making decisions that may foreseeably have a 
3 The District is listed as a "SDAC" -small disadvantaged community- referred to the California Rural Water 
Association for technical assistance. See htt s://www.waterboards.ca. ov/board info/a endas/20 12/dec/ l 20412 9c. df. 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 5 
material effect on any financial interest." This applies whether or not the employee's position has 
been properly designated by the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Regulation 187 46.1 ( a)( 4 ). ) 
At issue is whether your position involved "participating in making decisions." The Act 
defines "participating in a decision" in Regulation 18704(b) as follows: 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental 
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for 
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive 
review. 
"Making or participating in a decision" does not include actions by a public official that are solely 
ministerial, secretarial, or clerical. (Regulation 18704( d)( 1 ). ) 
In past advice letters, we have discussed "purely technical" actions by employees and 
consultants and how this type of work may fall into the exception for ministerial actions, even when 
those actions require considerable expertise and professional skill, if there is no discretion as to the 
outcome. In the Kaplan Advice Letter, No. A-82-108, we noted the exception does not apply where 
analyses or recommendations based on professional judgement are involved: 
However, this exception would not apply to technical tasks, such as most data 
gathering or analysis, in which the consultant makes professional judgments 
which can affect the ultimate decision in question. For example, ifthere are 
several "right" answers to a question, and a consultant not only does an analysis 
and calculation,. but also decides or advises as to which is the "best" right answer, 
his task is no longer ministerial. If the choice of which "right" answer to use will 
influence the governmental decision, the consultant is participating in the 
decision. 
Here, your position involved more than the gathering or calculation of data; it involved the 
analysis and recommendation of actions in response to the data. Your superiors relied on your 
reports, analyses and professional judgements. As such, you participated in these decisions. These 
decisions involved notices of violation or required corrective actions which, by their nature, had a 
foreseeable and material financial effect on the water facilities. You noted that findings of 
corrective actions had an additional financial effect on the water facilities, as the findings were 
sometimes used to justify their grant requests. 
The fact that your supervisor, a Senior Engineer, and his supervisor, a Principal Engineer, 
were the signatories on these letters does not alter your participation. We have advised that an 
employee participates in the making of a governmental decision, even if it is reviewed by several 
superiors, where the supervisor relies on the expertise or recommendation of the employee in 
making their decisions. (Lilyquist Advice Letter, No. M-96-318 [Memorandum to the Attorney 
General] and Gold Advice Letter, No. A-93-059.).) A showing of "significant intervening 
substantive review" of a recommendation is a narrowly applied exception and requires facts specific 
to a particular decision. Your facts indicate that, as a general matter, while your findings and 
recommendations were reviewed and discussed, your judgement was followed by your supervisors. 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 6 
Therefore, your position at the State Water Board, DDW, is subject to the one-year ban and the 
permanent ban, discussed below. 
The one-year ban applies to your employment with the Fall River District. For one year 
from the date of your separation from service with the State Water Board, you may not make an 
appearance or communication with the State Water Board, or any agency subject to its direction and 
control, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action; or any discretionary 
actions such as the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, grant or 
contract or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Regulation 18746.1.) 
Appearances and Communications for the Purpose of Influencing 
Regulation 18746.2 defines informal or formal appearances, as well as oral or written 
communications as "for the purpose of influencing" if the contact is made for the principal purpose 
of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or 
proceeding. These communications include, but are not limited to, conversing directly or by 
telephone, corresponding by writing or e-mail, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any 
communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).) 
However, you may engage in the following general types of appearances or 
communications: 
(1) Participate as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public 
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter 
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; 
(2) Attend a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event; 
(3) Request information concerning any matter of public record; or 
(4) Communicate with the press. 
(Regulation 18746.2(b)) 
In regards to your role in implementing the three "planning-level" grants that are currently 
in process, contacts with the State Water Board are permissible, but are restricted. Appearances or 
communications before a former state agency employer made as part of services performed to 
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or 
sale agreement are excluded from the prohibitions of Section 87406 and Regulation 18746.1, 
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of 
these actions or proceedings. (Regulation 187 46.1 ( c ). ) As an example, and assuming you did not 
participate in the proceeding as a state employee thereby triggering the permanent ban, the one-year 
ban would not prohibit you from contacting the State Water Board to clarify technical requirements 
under an awarded grant. However, you may not contact the State Water Board in relationship to the 
award of new or additional grant funds or the amendment of an existing grant. 
Without violating the one-year ban, you may also draft proposals on Fall River District's 
behalf to be submitted to the State Water Board, so long as you are not identified in connection with 
the District's efforts to influence the agency. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison Advice 
Letter, No. A-92-289.) Similarly, you may use your expertise to advise the District on the 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 7 
procedural requirements, plans, or policies of your former agency so long as you are not identified 
with the Fall River District's efforts to influence the State Water Board. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 
A-94-004.) · 
The Permanent Ban: 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from switching sides and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401, 87402.) The permanent ban 
applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of absence from any office or 
employment. (Regulation 18741.l(a)(l).) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication -- or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication -- made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated 'while you served as a state administrative official. These types of 
proceedings include: any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, 
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter 
involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency. (Section 87400(c).) 
Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims of specific parties. It 
does not apply to proceedings that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information .... " (Section 87400(d).) The permanent ban applies throughout the duration of a 
proceeding in which the official participated. 
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
(Sections 87401, 87402.) A "new" proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves 
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual or legal issues from those considered 
in previous proceedings. (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187 [superseded in part, relating to date of 
separation], Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the employee's former 
agency in which the former employee did not participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie 
Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is based on new consideration and new 
terms, even if it involves the same parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. 1-99-104.) 
You indicate that you had no prior involvement in the three active Fall River District grants 
while you were employed at DDW. Therefore, your facts do not raise a permanent ban issue as to 
these grants. Please contact our office if you require additional advice on other proceedings. 


File No. A-18-224 
Page No. 8 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
LKH:jgl 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
By: 
L. Karen Harrison 
Counsel, Legal Division

sections.question:
Are you subject to the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act due to your 
former position as a State Water Resources Control Board engineer?
sections.conclusion:
Yes. Your position involved participating in making decisions with foreseeable and material 
effects on financial interests, and you are subject to the one-year ban and the permanent ban under 
the Act. Based upon the facts provided, you may not contact or communicate with the State Water 
Board or any employee of the Board for the purposes of influencing an action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any contract or grant. However, theone-year ban does not prohibit limited communications with the State Water Board or an employee 
of the Board to implement an awarded grant. Permitted communications include seeking 
clarification of technical requirements identified in a grant that has already been awarded. 
Moreover, the one-year ban does not prohibit you from working on a grant on behalf of your 
current agency so long as you do not personally communicate or appear before the State Water 
Board, or an employee of the Board, and you are not identified by name in any documents 
submitted by your agency to the State Water Board related to the award of grant funds or the 
amendment of an existing grant.

sections.analysis:

employment provisions under the Act: the one-year ban and the permanent ban. (Sections 87400-
87406 and Regulation 18746.1.) 
The One-Year Ban 
Section 87406(d)(l) states: 
A designated employee of a state administrative agency, any officer, 
employee, or consultant of a state administrative agency who holds a position that 
entails the making, or participation in the making, of decisions that may 
foreseeably have a material effect on any financial interest, and a member of a 
state administrative agency, for a period of one year after leaving office or 
employment, shall not, for compensation, act as agent or attorney for, or 
otherwise represent, any other person, by making any formal or informal 
appearance, or by making any oral or written communication, before any state 
administrative agency, or officer or employee thereof, for which he or she worked 
or represented during the 12 months before leaving office or employment, if the 
appearance or communication is made for the purpose of influencing 
administrative or legislative action, or influencing any action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of a permit, license, 
grant, or contract, or the sale or purchase of goods or property ....

Unlike the permanent ban, this ban is not confined to particular proceedings. 
The determining factor in applying the one-year ban is whether the employee holds a 
position that "entails making or participating in making decisions that may foreseeably have a 
3 The District is listed as a "SDAC" -small disadvantaged community- referred to the California Rural Water 
Association for technical assistance. See htt s://www.waterboards.ca. ov/board info/a endas/20 12/dec/ l 20412 9c. df.  
material effect on any financial interest." This applies whether or not the employee's position has 
been properly designated by the agency's conflict-of-interest code. (Regulation 187 46.1 ( a)( 4 ). ) 
At issue is whether your position involved "participating in making decisions." The Act 
defines "participating in a decision" in Regulation 18704(b) as follows: 
(b) Participating in a Decision. A public official participates in a governmental 
decision if the official provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation for 
the purpose of affecting the decision without significant intervening substantive 
review. 
"Making or participating in a decision" does not include actions by a public official that are solely 
ministerial, secretarial, or clerical. (Regulation 18704( d)( 1 ). ) 
In past advice letters, we have discussed "purely technical" actions by employees and

decision. 
Here, your position involved more than the gathering or calculation of data; it involved the 
analysis and recommendation of actions in response to the data. Your superiors relied on your

Therefore, your position at the State Water Board, DDW, is subject to the one-year ban and the 
permanent ban, discussed below. 
The one-year ban applies to your employment with the Fall River District. For one year

appearance or communication with the State Water Board, or any agency subject to its direction and 
control, for the purpose of influencing any legislative or administrative action; or any discretionary 
actions such as the issuance, amendment, awarding or revocation of a permit, license, grant or 
contract or the sale or purchase of goods or property. (Regulation 18746.1.) 
Appearances and Communications for the Purpose of Influencing 
Regulation 18746.2 defines informal or formal appearances, as well as oral or written 
communications as "for the purpose of influencing" if the contact is made for the principal purpose 
of supporting, promoting, influencing, modifying, opposing, delaying, or advancing the action or 
proceeding. These communications include, but are not limited to, conversing directly or by 
telephone, corresponding by writing or e-mail, attending a meeting, and delivering or sending any 
communication. (Regulation 18746.2(a).) 
However, you may engage in the following general types of appearances or 
communications: 
(1) Participate as a panelist or formal speaker at a conference or similar public 
event for educational purposes or to disseminate research and the subject matter 
does not pertain to a specific action or proceeding; 
(2) Attend a general informational meeting, seminar, or similar event; 
(3) Request information concerning any matter of public record; or 
(4) Communicate with the press. 
(Regulation 18746.2(b)) 
In regards to your role in implementing the three "planning-level" grants that are currently 
in process, contacts with the State Water Board are permissible, but are restricted. Appearances or 
communications before a former state agency employer made as part of services performed to 
administer, implement, or fulfill the requirements of an existing permit, license, grant, contract, or 
sale agreement are excluded from the prohibitions of Section 87406 and Regulation 18746.1, 
provided the services do not involve the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any of 
these actions or proceedings. (Regulation 187 46.1 ( c ). ) As an example, and assuming you did not 
participate in the proceeding as a state employee thereby triggering the permanent ban, the one-year 
ban would not prohibit you from contacting the State Water Board to clarify technical requirements 
under an awarded grant. However, you may not contact the State Water Board in relationship to the 
award of new or additional grant funds or the amendment of an existing grant. 
Without violating the one-year ban, you may also draft proposals on Fall River District's 
behalf to be submitted to the State Water Board, so long as you are not identified in connection with 
the District's efforts to influence the agency. (Cook Advice Letter, No. A-95-321; Harrison Advice 
Letter, No. A-92-289.) Similarly, you may use your expertise to advise the District on the  
procedural requirements, plans, or policies of your former agency so long as you are not identified 
with the Fall River District's efforts to influence the State Water Board. (Perry Advice Letter, No. 
A-94-004.) · 
The Permanent Ban: 
The permanent ban prohibits a former state employee from switching sides and 
participating, for compensation, in a certain proceeding involving the State of California and other 
specific parties, or assisting in the proceeding if the proceeding is one in which the former state 
employee participated while employed by the state. (Sections 87401, 87402.) The permanent ban 
applies when an official has permanently left or takes a leave of absence from any office or 
employment. (Regulation 18741.l(a)(l).) 
The permanent ban is a lifetime ban and applies to any formal or informal appearance or any 
oral or written communication -- or aiding, advising, counseling, consulting, or assisting in 
representing any other person, other than the State of California, in an appearance or 
communication -- made with the intent to influence any judicial, quasi-judicial, or other proceeding 
in which you participated 'while you served as a state administrative official. These types of 
proceedings include: any proceeding, application, request for a ruling or other determination, 
contract, claim, controversy, investigation, charge, accusation, arrest or other particular matter 
involving a specific party or parties in any court or state administrative agency. (Section 87400(c).) 
Thus, the permanent ban covers proceedings that affect the rights or claims of specific parties. It 
does not apply to proceedings that involve the making of rules or policies of general applicability. 
An official is considered to have "participated" in a proceeding if he or she took part in the 
proceeding "personally, and substantially through decision, approval, disapproval, formal written 
recommendation, rendering advice on a substantial basis, investigation, or use of confidential 
information .... " (Section 87400(d).) The permanent ban applies throughout the duration of a 
proceeding in which the official participated. 
The permanent ban does not apply to a "new" proceeding even in cases where the new 
proceeding is related to or grows out of a prior proceeding in which the official had participated. 
(Sections 87401, 87402.) A "new" proceeding not subject to the permanent ban typically involves 
different parties, a different subject matter, or different factual or legal issues from those considered 
in previous proceedings. (Rist Advice Letter, No. A-04-187 [superseded in part, relating to date of 
separation], Donovan Advice Letter, No. I-03-119.) New contracts with the employee's former 
agency in which the former employee did not participate are considered new proceedings. (Leslie 
Advice Letter, No. I-89-649.) A new contract is one that is based on new consideration and new 
terms, even if it involves the same parties. (Ferber Advice Letter, No. 1-99-104.) 
You indicate that you had no prior involvement in the three active Fall River District grants 
while you were employed at DDW. Therefore, your facts do not raise a permanent ban issue as to 
these grants. Please contact our office if you require additional advice on other proceedings.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Are you subject to the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act due to your 
former position as a State Water Resources Control Board engineer?

CONCLUSION: Yes. Your position involved participating in making decisions with foreseeable and material 
effects on financial interests, and you are subject to the one-year ban and the permanent ban under 
the Act. Based upon the facts provided, you may not contact or communicate with the State Water 
Board or any employee of the Board for the purposes of influencing an action or proceeding 
involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any contract or grant. However, theone-year ban does not prohibit limited communications with the State Water Board or an employee 
of the Board to implement an awarded grant. Permitted communications include seeking 
clarification of technical requirements identified in a grant that has already been awarded. 
Moreover, the one-year ban does not prohibit you from working on a grant on behalf of your 
current agency so long as you do not personally communicate or appear before the State Water 
Board, or an employee of the Board, and you are not identified by name in any documents 
submitted by your agency to the State Water Board related to the award of grant funds or the 
amendment of an existing grant.

Rooklidge, PhD, PE General Manager November 6, 2018 Fall River Valley Community Services District PO Box 427 Fall River Mills, CA 96028 Re: Your Request for Advice Our File No. A-18-224 Dear Mr. Rooklidge: This letter responds to your request for advice regarding post-government provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 Please note that we are only providing advice under the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act. We therefore offer no opinion on the application, if any, of other post- governmental employment laws that may apply, such as Public Contract Code Section 10411, or Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby (1975) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us for additional advice. QUESTION Are you subject to the post-governmental employment provisions of the Act due to your former position as a State Water Resources Control Board engineer? CONCLUSION Yes. Your position involved participating in making decisions with foreseeable and material effects on financial interests, and you are subject to the one-year ban and the permanent ban under the Act. Based upon the facts provided, you may not contact or communicate with the State Water Board or any employee of the Board for the purposes of influencing an action or proceeding involving the issuance, amendment, awarding, or revocation of any contract or grant. However, the 1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. File No. A-18-224 Page No. 2 one-year ban does not prohibit limited communications with the State Water Board or an employee of the Board to implement an awarded grant. Permitted communications include seeking clarification of technical requirements identified in a grant that has already been awarded. Moreover, the one-year ban does not prohibit you from working on a grant on behalf of your current agency so long as you do not personally communicate or appear before the State Water Board, or an employee of the Board, and you are not identified by name in any documents submitted by your agency to the State Water Board related to the award of grant funds or the amendment of an existing grant. FACTS AS PRESENTED BY REQUESTER In August, 2018 you left state service. You were previously employed by the State Water Resources Control Board ("State Water Board"), Division of Drinking Water ("DDW"), Northern
